sentences.text: When-- when we went out of the ghetto, my brother-- my dad said, take your brother.
And we went to-- to the southern-- the East-Southern Poland, to a city we were planning, Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski.
Because Mayer had relatives there, that they had connections with farmers.
We went there.
And for the first time, when I looked out the windows, I saw Jews waiting for transport.
It was raining.
Little children, and they were waiting.
I just put my nose to the-- to the window, because I have never seen that before.
And--
No.
Just they were waiting, there, were waiting.
And we went-- we went to-- I had to go to one place then change the train, and to go to Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski.
We come to Ostrowiec Swietokrzyski, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] already the Jews are being sent out.
When me and my brother went toward the city-- because it was outside the city-- the Polish workers said, go back.
They're killing the Jews.
They're taking out the Jews from the ghetto.
And I was thinking, how did they know that we were Jews?
We come back.
We go sit in the train station.
And we wait to get back to Warsaw.
Because where am I going to go?
I want to go to Warsaw and get in   touch with Tosia Altman.
But when I wait there-- She was your friend?
She was a member of the [POLISH], of the central committee.
Of the Jew-- of the Hashomer Hatzair, yes.
And she looked, more or less, like a Polish girl.
So I was going to go to-- to see her.
And when we stay in the line for a ticket, I send my brother first.
He gets the ticket.
And I stood a little behind him.
And then, a Polish man comes over to me and says like this, you want to get a ticket?
You better pay.
If you don't pay, you don't get a ticket.
And I learned already, you open a big mouth.
And I told him [POLISH]-- in Polish, [POLISH].
Go to devil.
Did you count my money?
How do you know I have money?
And if you don't-- just-- just threaten the one will threatens you.
So I-- I told him, if you don't lay off of me, I am going to go right over-- you see the policemens that are going here?
I will tell them that you wanted to-- to get money from-- from me.
I am close to the window, and I will get my ticket.
And he was afraid.
It was such a traumatic thing, to wait so many hours in the-- in the train station.
And-- in [POLISH], young hooligans, you know, teenagers were just making, you know, like this, different finger motions.
And I just got at them back and did the same thing.
Finally, we did get the ticket.
We get the ticket.
I go on the train.
And I-- our-- our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --link in Warsaw, from Hashomer Hatzair, from the underground, was a Polish scout master.
Her name was Irina Adamowitch.
She loved Hashomer Hatzair.
She loved the Zionists.
And she always knew our mail was coming to her house.
So I went there.
In Warsaw.
So I went to her place, where the letters were going, with my brother.
And she wasn't home.
Her aunt was there.
And her aunt didn't want me to-- she said, she's in the orphanage!
And I went into the bathroom to wash myself real nice.
I'm not going to go into details.
It was so peaceful, so clean from all those things that we went through in two days.
I go.
She's not there, in the orphanage.
Then I found out, in the orphanage, that she is still in her office in-- in-- in Warsaw.
I run fast with my brother.
And we run those steps up.
And she was there.
She was there.
And she had a meeting with somebody which was from the Jewish underground.
I didn't know that girl.
And she told me to wait.
And when I came into her office, I just couldn't hold it any longer.
I start to cry.
And we had a word-- a-- a clue word.
I told her the family is sick.
And she knew what that meant.
And she gave me the telephone number to call into the ghetto.
And I knew only in the ghetto the man who used to come to us.
Vurrer ?] was his name.
So I go, ring the telephone.
And I say, can you-- I would like to speak [?
Vurrer. ?] It was quiet on the other side.
And they said, [?
Vurrer ?] is not here, and who are you?
I told them that I am from the family in Piotrkow.
And then, [?
Vurrer ?] is not there.
I call another time.
Somebody else answers.
And then they make appointment with me in Warsaw, winter, cold, frozen, that they will come on-- [?
Aleya-- ?] [?
Wiesdow-- ?] I don't know if it's-- or Piotrkowska, I don't remember exactly the street, by the train-- by the tram station.
I go there with my brother.
And I told them what I'm going to wear, angora hat, a gray-- gray coat with a black fur collar.
I am going back and forth, back and forth.
Nobody is there to contact me.
I got very angry.
I went back toward the hotel where I was in.
And I call them back.
I said, look, you told me to come.
I came there.
And nobody-- so he said, ah, because you were with a man there.
See, my brother was with me.
And you didn't say that you were there with a man.
So I said, this was my brother.
So finally, they made me another appointment.
And that's how I met Tosia Altman.
And Tosia Altman told me a few things.
First, you have to find a job.
Then, you'll got legalized-- you know, your permit to live, once you work.
And then, when you will have everything in order, then you get in touch with me.
How do you get a job?
From a newspaper.
So she asked me if I am afraid.
Well, anyhow, I went back to Cracow.
Because in Warsaw, it was impossible to get a job.
And I was afraid.
There were so many-- they called them [POLISH].
And lots of Jews were hiding, like me, that they went over and-- and just skimmed every man, every thing from them.
So I didn't--
And Tosia Altman says, you afraid?
No.
Go to Cracow.
There is a Jewish girl in Cracow.
And she told me where she works.
Go get in touch with her.
So I go, go to Cracow.
But when I am on the train, and the train comes in, in the main station in Warsaw, a man sits across.
And I know this is my cousin.
Guterman was his name.
I don't remember his first name.
And I was afraid.
We sits in those-- in those compartments.
So I changed the place of somebody who sit next to him.
And I said, let me-- let me talk with this man, because I know him.
And I was afraid he didn't know what my name was, that he will do something.
So I sit there.
And I-- you know, I elbowed him.
And I said, hi.
Remember me, Wanda Gajda?
And that's how I gave him the clue what my new name is.
Where are you going?
He said.
I told him I'm going to-- to Cracow.
He offered to go with me.
Because he has places that I will sure find a place where to stay.
And so we went to Cracow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
No, my brother is on a job somewhere by Warsaw.
By myself.
I see two-- two Selbschutzes, with the German blue, Volksdeutscher, and somebody else.
And they're gesticulating and talking.
And I know they're talking about me, because he's not bashful.
So I said, like that-- I go get a newspaper.
I buy the newspaper and I look, you know, with the-- with the-- with the corner of my eyes to prepare myself.
And they talk, came over to me.
And he said like this, this German Volksdeutsche-- one was a Polack who was just fishing out all the Jews.
And he says like this, you-- where are you going?
He ask me, this Volksdeutsche.
I said, I am going to Piotrkow.
What you doing here?
I'm waiting for a train.
So he said like this, this Polack-- calls over another Volksdeutsche.
And he says like this, listen, this woman says she's going to Piotrkow.
Which mean she is from [POLISH], you know, parish.
So he ask me, what is my parish?
And I told him.
Because there was Dafka, a Catholic priest who was a brother, when my brother went to-- to Polish schools he was teaching there.
So I told him the [POLISH].
And I told him the name of the [POLISH] or-- I don't remember.
And he said, what do you want from her?
She is from my parish.
I know-- and I told him-- I gave him my false address, where-- where-- what it says on my paper.
And he said, you know what?
I will be on leave very soon, and I will come to visit you.
I said, wonderful.
Come.
He wrote my address and show-- now, how do you-- when you go out from a situation like this, you have to be cool.
You have not to show any emotions.
Because he didn't have-- if he would have touched my heart, it was going like this.
I go down.
And I see my cousin coming from shaving.
Because in Cracow, the train station was a little higher, quite a few steps.
And I make to him like this.
And he already knew he cannot come to see me, because there is something.
I went down, down the street.
And then, he followed me when nobody-- I bend down to tie my shoe.
When nobody followed, he came over.
And he told me, you know, this-- let's go to this place, [INAUDIBLE] 6, and maybe you will get, there, a place where to sleep.
So I went there.
She didn't want to get anybody.
And I found the-- the woman from Warsaw, from Hashomer Hatzair.
She was waiting for somebody else, not for me.
And I got to be friends with this woman, who owned this place.
And said, you know what?
I like you.
You will stay here, and one of my roomers is moving out.
And I will give him-- give you his corner, his bed.
It wasn't a room.
It was just a bed.
So that's what happened.
I stayed there on [INAUDIBLE] 6 and--
I didn't have a job yet.
So what happened, her sister and the other neighbors there said that they go to the [POLISH] to get a job.
I was going every day.
There was no job.
One day-- I was going, maybe three weeks or two weeks-- there sits there, the secretary, dark hair.
And she said, you know, I like you.
You are farther on the list to get a job.
This girl, she doesn't come.
She's not serious.
Looks to me that you want to work, so I'm giving you her job.
She said, ?] do you have muscle?
So I got her job.
And I started to work in a hospital.
In the hospital, and my first title was the stair-lady, the one who cleans the stairs.
There were many stairs to clean.
And even to this, there is a trick.
So I worked there.
And then, I got fired.
Mm-hmm.
That mean-- meant that, if you're on the run, you hide next to the police.
I worked for the Germans, in the hospital.
And this was-- but this was not really yet, in the mouth of the wolf.
He said, you have to hide in the mouth of the wolf, under the official's nose, and watch that they don't want to devour you.
When I was fired-- because I did some 'good deed,' so to speak, for one of the Polish ladies.
I was translating to a Russian soldier.
She wanted to buy a blanket.
Then, I was called to-- to the the Montelupich in Cracow.
This is a-- a political prison.
And when I came there, the-- the men asked what you doing?
He was starting and-- telling me stuff, something.
Anyhow, I make-- make humor.
He said, we going to lock you-- oh, now I remember-- in the room with-- with-- and you will be talking.
Because this other Polish girl bought blankets.
So I told him, listen.
I said, are you going to put me by myself or with a few nice-looking guys?
Because if you put me in, put me in with a few nice guys, so at least we will be fun.
And he liked that.
You know?
And he said that I'm not lying, because you know, I came back right away.
I said, listen, I didn't-- I just translated for this woman to this Russian soldier.
And this Russian soldier was a Grusian.
And I think he-- he was on drugs, with pocked face.
And every time, when I was going with the dishes of-- of water to wash the dishes, he was following me.
And lots of Russian were-- were already on the Vlasov's army.
And I told him like this, [RUSSIAN], if you want to talk to me, you have to put your hands in the pocket.
Otherwise, you cannot talk to me.
And-- and that's what I told.
And the other didn't want to let me go.
And the soldiers, I had, already, my reputation that Wanda doesn't flirt with the soldiers.
Leave her alone.
So when he came and was [? chapping ?] with me, and I was going with the warm water, a basin like this, I turned around and I put the water on him.
It wasn't hot.
It was warm.
And he said, you will pay for it horridly.
And he went to the-- to the director, the German of the hospital.
He said, this man said that you are selling--
OK.
Once I was laid off from the hospital, I cannot find a job.
I go every day to the unemployment office.
I cannot find a job.
Then one neighbor where I was living, a Polish man, said, you know, I work for the SS, he said.
And they always need people.
But they don't take them from the unemployment office.
You have to go there, register yourself.
And then you will go work for the SS.
I could have sat there in the unemployment office forever, I wouldn't have gotten a job.
So when he told me that, he told me where the office was.
I went to the office.
I registered.
And I said that I want a job.
What kind of job?
Any kind of job here.
I want it in the kitchen.
Because-- so he ask me, why in the kitchen?
I said, because at least you-- you can eat when you work in the kitchen.
So they gave me a job, that my first job was to clean the stairs.
No, scrub potatoes or clean the stairs, one of those.
And I was sitting there and working.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --one day, Mr.
Albert, he was the commandant of the Third Battalion for the recruit Ausbildung Battalion.
And his name was Kurt Albert.
He was bringing his wife to Cracow.
The wife was waiting to have a child.
And he went to Mr.
Meyer and said, I need a Polack girl who doesn't smoke, who doesn't drink, and doesn't flirt with the soldiers.
So they came out, there's one girl, Wanda.
So he say-- he already knew me.
I didn't know.
Because sometimes I was cleaning the offices for the officers.
He says, oh!
This is Wanda, who kills the operas?
You know, I-- the-- the operettas.
OK.
One day I'm being called.
Two soldiers-- or was it one?
It was, you know, a helmet and just like this, and a gun with a bayonet-- takes me.
I have never gone to the third floor.
He said, now you stay.
He put me through the office of the adjutant.
Adjutant rings the bell.
The Wanda is here.
Hmm?
And how.
Because what did this guy want from me, you know, with-- I go in.
And Mr.
Albert doesn't even look at me.
The room was spacious.
On the walls and on the doors, there were cushions, sound proof.
That whatever comes out there, whatever you speak, nobody can hear from the outside.
He was looking at a-- a king's castle.
And he asked me like this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do-- do you know first-aid?
I said, yes, I know.
How do you know first-aid?
I said, before the war, in high school, we have to have a course in first-aid.
I can bandage.
I can do-- do a elbow bandage, a head bandage.
OK.
And he ask me something else.
I don't remember what it-- what you hear and see, you will never tell to anybody!
I said, no.
And OK.
He didn't tell me what.
I was shaking.
And he asked me how I know German, some other things like this.
So he called the adjutant.
Take her-- take her out.
I went down.
And when I went to Mr.
Meyer, he said, listen.
Albert is bringing his wife.
He wants a Polish girl to take care of the baby and to clean the house.
So you're going to be it.
And that how I started to work from there.
Because he was a higher officer.
So he could take everybody and-- to his-- as a help-- help to his home.
He was-- well, he was having a silver braids and two stars on the braids.
I don't remember what it was called.
And he didn't even pay me.
The SS-- I was on the-- on the payroll list from the SS.
Every Friday--
Well, yes.
I didn't get any money from the unemployment.
So I was getting money from them.
And I think I had to go every time.
I think every second Friday, or whatever, I had to go and get my check.
So that's--
The wife's name was-- wait a second.
Mayer, do you remember her name?
He was Kurt.
And-- oh, wait. it's-- it's on the tongue!
The baby's name was [?
Klaus. ?] And Klaus was born with a-- with a-- with a club foot.
I don't know if you know what it is, that the foot is-- so I was supposed-- yeah, the wife's name was [?
Taya. ?] [?
Taya. ?] And I was supposed to do-- my job was to clean the house, to wash [?
Klaus, ?] to make massages on the-- on this club foot, to go with him, [? spazieren. ?] And this was the best place for me.
Because I was always, by the Polacks, harassed that she's Jewish.
I-- I don't want, even, to tell you how many incidents I had.
But when I was going with Klaus on Bernadinska Street to the gardens, I was speaking to Klaus, German.
And I was going in-- it was called the German Kaserne.
They let me in.
I signed my name in.
And I stayed there with the-- with the baby.
And then, when time was for his food or whatever, I went back home.
And I will tell you, that I never knew-- see, when you're on the run, you have to know.
I already-- when I was in the Bernardino Kaserne, this was a-- a church on the Bernardino Street.
And the-- and the fathers had a vegetable garden.
And on one side was a big fence.
I already looked, in case something happened, where will be my route to run away?
It'd be better to go through the-- through the church or through the side gate?
And every time, I was wearing on my body-- body, 50 zlotys and my passport-- because I already changed the passport-- rolled in, you know, from this-- a pink passport, because I figured it like this.
If something happened, I will run.
Once I have a-- a passport and 50 zloty, I will be OK.
So that how it was.
And I also forgot, when I didn't get the job-- six weeks, I think, or three weeks-- I was running on the trains, from one train to the other.
From one, I was waiting in the-- in the train stations, covered myself up, sit there, and went on another train.
First, that it was safer, cheaper, and easier to spend the time.
I don't--
I don't think that I am looking over my shoulder.
See, I figured like this.
You were born in the wrong times in history.
You are a actress.
You have to play your role good.
Because you have to pay one price, it is your life.
And-- and-- and I didn't want to just to give my life away for nothing.
And when the war was over, I right away started to work with Mayer-- he came back from the concentration camp-- with children.
And we did Aliyah.
We sent them on Aliyah Bet and different things.
So but I never looked, really, over-- because I wasn't-- this was my role, to be a actress.
No.
But you know what-- what I did?
When I worked here in the Hebrew school, I organized all the plays.
I could read Shalom Aleichem's story.
And I changed the script to a play.
And I was training the children.
And we had many, many plays, you know?
Playettes, what you'll say-- made costumes.
And that was my unfulfilled actress business.
How long did you stay with the Alberts?
I stayed with the Alberts until-- from 19-- [SIGHS] I stayed till the end of the war.
But I don't remember-- I know I came there, to their apartment, Christmas, 1941 or 1940-- no--
Yes.
And-- and I stayed till the end.
And I already wanted to run away, to go to Germany as a Polish worker.
But they didn't let me go.
Albert said, you can get another Polack to-- to go and work.
Wanda, she takes care of our child.
And so they didn't let me go.
You know?
Because [?
Klaus ?] loved you.
Yeah, [?
Klaus ?] loved me.
And they could go everywhere.
I only also didn't mention a few things.
When they went out to dinners, they knew that I was treating the son, the baby, well.
